name: Western
description: Stories set on an untamed frontier where individual agency, physical competence, and a personal moral code are the only reliable sources of order in a world where law is absent or corrupt. The Western dramatizes the cost of survival and the question of what kind of person — and what kind of civilization — emerges when violence and justice must be negotiated without institutions.

nodes.label: Western Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story in which an individual confronts the frontier on its own terms — where survival demands physical competence, moral reckoning is personal rather than institutional, and the landscape itself is an antagonist. The Western promises that agency matters, that violence has weight, and that the line between civilization and wildness will be tested and possibly redrawn by the end.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as Western genre
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Classic frontier gunfighter Western
Settler/homesteader survival story
Revisionist Western interrogating the myth
Contemporary/neo-Western transposing frontier dynamics to modern settings
failure_modes: Frontier setting used as backdrop for a story that could take place anywhere
Violence is spectacle without moral or physical consequence
Protagonist's agency is irrelevant — outcomes are predetermined by external forces
signals_in_text: Frontier landscape is introduced as a shaping force, not decoration
Protagonist's competence and code are established early
The absence or failure of institutional law is made clear

nodes.label: Frontier Setting as Active Force
nodes.definition: The frontier — whether the American West, a borderland, a lawless territory, or its contemporary equivalent — must function as an active shaping force on the story, not as background atmosphere. In the Western, geography determines what is possible: distances are real, weather kills, water is scarce, and the land does not accommodate human weakness. The frontier is not merely where the story happens; it is part of what the story is about.
exit_conditions: Setting actively constrains and shapes character choices; the landscape cannot be removed without collapsing the story's logic
typical_variants: Desert Southwest with heat and water scarcity as constant pressure
Mountain passes and winter as mortal obstacles
Open plains where visibility cuts both ways
Small frontier town as the fragile edge of civilization
failure_modes: Frontier is decorative — characters move through it without being shaped or constrained by it
Landscape is never hostile or indifferent; it conveniently supports the plot
signals_in_text: Characters make decisions based on landscape reality — distance, water, terrain
Weather and environment create plot complications
The land's indifference to human survival is felt throughout

nodes.label: Personal Moral Code Substitutes for Law
nodes.definition: In the absence of reliable institutional law, the Western protagonist operates according to a personal moral code — an internalized standard of honor, loyalty, and justice that may or may not align with society's nominal rules. This code is the protagonist's true law, and the story tests it: whether it holds under pressure, whether it is adequate to the situation, and what it costs to maintain or violate it. The Western's moral drama is always about the individual's code, not the society's statutes.
entry_conditions: Institutional law is absent, corrupt, or inadequate
exit_conditions: Protagonist's personal code is articulated through action and tested by events; the code has consequences — following it costs something, violating it costs something
typical_variants: Gunfighter's code — honor in combat, loyalty to one's word
Lawman's code — justice over revenge, even when justice is painful
Outlaw's code — loyalty to the gang above all outside law
Settler's code — endurance, community, refusal to abandon the land
failure_modes: Protagonist has no discernible code — acts on pure self-interest throughout
Code is stated but never tested — the story never forces the protagonist to pay for their principles
Code is merely the society's laws restated — no tension between personal and institutional justice
signals_in_text: Protagonist makes a costly choice based on principle rather than self-interest
Code is revealed through what the protagonist refuses to do as much as what they do
Other characters recognize and respond to the protagonist's code — it has social reality

nodes.label: Physical Competence Determines Outcomes
nodes.definition: The Western insists that physical skill — with weapons, horses, the land, and one's own body — is not incidental but determinative. On the frontier, competence keeps you alive and incompetence gets you killed. This constraint elevates the body as a site of meaning: what a person can do physically expresses who they are, and the gap between the skilled and the unskilled is a genuine gap in power and survival. The Western's respect for competence is part of its moral argument.
exit_conditions: Physical competence is shown to matter — skilled characters have advantages, mistakes cost lives, the climax turns on capability
typical_variants: Gunfighter skill as the central measure of power
Tracking and survival skills that determine who finds whom first
Horsemanship that enables or forecloses escape and pursuit
Knowledge of terrain that tilts tactical advantage
failure_modes: Physical incompetence has no consequences — unskilled characters survive as easily as skilled ones
Competence is asserted in dialogue but never demonstrated under pressure
The protagonist's skill is irrelevant to the story's resolution
signals_in_text: Specific skills are shown, not just claimed — training, demonstration, or display under pressure
Incompetence carries consequences — injury, capture, death
The climactic confrontation requires and reveals the protagonist's mastery

nodes.label: Law Is Absent, Weak, or Corrupt
nodes.definition: The Western requires that institutional law — sheriffs, courts, armies, governments — be absent, insufficient, corrupt, or captured by power. This absence is what creates the frontier's moral vacuum and forces individuals to be their own law. Without this constraint, the Western collapses into a procedural or civic drama where institutions solve problems; with it, every act of justice or violence is a personal moral choice with no external validation or sanction.
exit_conditions: Institutional law is shown to be insufficient or unavailable; characters cannot rely on external authority to resolve the central conflict
typical_variants: No law at all — frontier territory beyond any jurisdiction
Sheriff who is too weak or too scared to act
Law that is bought by the powerful and serves them, not justice
Law that arrives too late — the individual must act before institutions can
failure_modes: Functioning institutions resolve the central conflict — the protagonist is essentially waiting for the law to do its job
The law's absence is mentioned but never felt — institutions keep solving problems quietly in the background
signals_in_text: Protagonist explicitly cannot or does not rely on legal authority to solve the problem
Antagonist operates with impunity because law cannot or will not stop them
The resolution of the central conflict is achieved by individual action, not institutional process

nodes.label: Classic/Traditional Western
nodes.definition: The archetypal Western form: a skilled, morally grounded protagonist — often a gunfighter, lawman, or stranger — rides into a community threatened by lawlessness and, through a combination of competence, code, and confrontation, restores a fragile order before departing or settling. The Classic Western affirms the frontier myth — that individual virtue and skill can carve justice from chaos — and does so without irony. Its moral world is legible: the code is real, the threat is genuine, the confrontation is earned.
entry_conditions: Narrative affirms rather than interrogates the frontier myth
Protagonist's code is essentially sound and the story endorses it
exit_conditions: Threat is neutralized through individual competence and moral resolve; the protagonist's code is vindicated, even at personal cost
typical_variants: The stranger who comes to town and cleans it up
The lawman who stands alone against the outlaw
The rancher defending land and family against a land-grabber
The bounty hunter who tracks his quarry across the landscape
failure_modes: Classic form executed without moral weight — competence without code
The threat is resolved without genuine cost or risk to the protagonist
The confrontation is anticlimactic — no real test of the protagonist's resolve
signals_in_text: Protagonist's moral superiority is established through behavior, not just words
Antagonist's threat is credible and has already inflicted real harm before the climax
Showdown is the story's dramatic culmination, not an interruption of it

nodes.label: Revisionist Western
nodes.definition: The Revisionist Western uses the genre's conventions to interrogate, complicate, or dismantle the frontier myth — exposing the violence, dispossession, and moral compromise that the Classic Western's heroic frame glosses over. It does not simply add darkness to the Classic form; it restructures the moral architecture of the story so that the hero's code may be inadequate, compromised, or implicated in the same violence it claims to oppose. The revisionist mode demands that the audience reckon with the real costs of frontier civilization.
entry_conditions: Narrative stance interrogates or complicates the frontier myth
The protagonist's code or the genre's conventional moral clarity is put under pressure
exit_conditions: Story produces moral ambiguity, tragedy, or disillusionment; the genre's conventions are shown to have a cost the Classic form would not acknowledge
typical_variants: Hero whose violence is indistinguishable from the villain's
Frontier conquest told from the perspective of the dispossessed
Aging gunfighter whose competence is now merely the capacity for destruction
The law as instrument of land theft rather than justice
failure_modes: Revisionism is only aesthetic — the story is darker but makes the same moral argument as the Classic Western
Deconstruction without genuine moral stakes — nihilism rather than reckoning
Every Western convention is subverted but nothing is said in their place
signals_in_text: The genre's heroic conventions are invoked and then complicated or betrayed
Violence has consequences that the Classic Western would absorb without comment
Protagonist's code is shown to be self-serving, insufficient, or implicated in injustice

nodes.label: Frontier/Settler Western
nodes.definition: The Settler Western centers not on the gunfighter or lawman but on the ordinary people trying to build something permanent on the frontier — homesteaders, farmers, town-builders, women, families — whose survival depends on endurance, community, and the slow work of civilization-making. This subgenre's drama is in the cost of staying: the land, the weather, the violence, the isolation, and the question of whether what is being built is worth what it requires.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is engaged in the project of settlement rather than individual conflict resolution
exit_conditions: Story dramatizes the long-form cost of building on the frontier — what is lost, what is gained, and whether the enterprise is sustainable or just
typical_variants: Homesteaders fighting drought, hard winters, and hostile land
Town-builders navigating the tension between frontier violence and civic order
Women holding a community together when men are killed or absent
Settler community versus powerful interests that want the land
failure_modes: Settlement is treated as given — the hardship of frontier life is not shown
Community exists as background only — no interpersonal dynamics, no civic drama
Protagonist can leave at any time — no genuine stake in the place they are building
signals_in_text: Physical labor of survival and settlement is shown, not summarized
Community members are individuated and their relationships matter
The land resists settlement — it is not easily domesticated

nodes.label: Contemporary/Neo-Western
nodes.definition: The Neo-Western transposes frontier dynamics — absent law, personal code, physical competence as survival, landscape as antagonist — to contemporary or non-historical settings, recognizing that the frontier is a condition rather than a time and place. Its protagonists inhabit modern borderlands, rural margins, criminal economies, or institutional voids where the same pressures apply: law is unavailable or corrupt, violence is a real option, and survival requires the kind of individual moral reckoning the Classical Western performed in a different costume.
entry_conditions: Frontier dynamics are structurally present — absent law, physical stakes, personal code — even if the surface is modern
exit_conditions: Story's contemporary setting maps coherently onto frontier constraints — the modern world is shown to have its own frontiers where the Western's moral logic applies
typical_variants: Crime thriller in a lawless rural landscape
Drug war narrative as frontier conflict
Ex-soldier or outlaw navigating a world where institutions have failed them
Modern bounty hunter or investigator in a land beyond law
failure_modes: Contemporary setting but the frontier constraints are absent — institutions function normally, law is available, physical competence is irrelevant
Neo-Western is only an aesthetic — Western iconography without Western logic
signals_in_text: Modern landscape functions as frontier — isolated, hostile, beyond reliable law
Protagonist's code operates in the absence of institutional moral authority
Physical competence or toughness is a real determinant of survival

nodes.label: Landscape Functions as Character
nodes.definition: In the Western, the physical environment must be rendered with enough specificity and agency that it functions as a character in the drama — not merely as backdrop but as a force with its own will and consequences. The desert does not care if you run out of water; the mountains do not yield to urgency; the land imposes its own logic on human plans. This setting rule requires that the landscape shape scenes at the level of cause and effect, not just ambiance.
exit_conditions: At least one scene turns on landscape-as-cause: the environment creates a problem, forecloses an option, or determines an outcome
typical_variants: Water scarcity as a plot engine — where water is determines where characters can go
Mountains or canyons that funnel movement and create tactical inevitability
Heat and exposure as a clock — characters must move or die
Winter and cold as antagonist — freezing threatens the journey or the homestead
failure_modes: Landscape is described lyrically but never affects plot or character choices
Characters cross vast distances without food, water, or weather being relevant
The environment is always conveniently passable when the plot requires movement
signals_in_text: Specific landscape features — a dry riverbed, a mountain pass, a canyon — determine tactical options
Characters carry, ration, or run out of resources tied to the landscape
Journey scenes include the actual difficulty of frontier travel

nodes.label: Limited Resources and Isolation Shape All Choices
nodes.definition: The frontier's defining material condition is that help is far away, resources are finite, and the consequences of error accumulate without the relief of institutional support. This setting rule requires that the story dramatize isolation as a real constraint: you cannot send for a doctor in a day, you cannot get more ammunition before the siege, you cannot be rescued by outside authority in time. Isolation is not just atmosphere — it closes options and forces the protagonist to work with what they have.
exit_conditions: At least one critical scene turns on the unavailability of resources or assistance; characters must solve problems with what they have rather than what they could summon
typical_variants: Nearest town is three days' ride — medical help will not arrive in time
Ammunition is finite and the siege is long
Winter has cut off the homestead from all supply
Protagonist is wounded and must self-treat in the field
failure_modes: Reinforcements and supplies arrive conveniently whenever needed
The nearest town or help is always reachable in the story's timeline
Wounds and injuries are treated with implausibly full resources
signals_in_text: Characters explicitly acknowledge the absence of external help and plan accordingly
Resource scarcity creates decision pressure — what to use now versus what to save
Distance and time are treated as real constraints, not narrative conveniences

nodes.label: The Frontier Code Governs Conduct
nodes.definition: Beyond the individual protagonist's personal code, the Western requires that a shared frontier code — unwritten but understood — govern the conduct of those who live in this world. The code covers hospitality (water and shelter are given without question), fair dealing (a man's word binds), respect for certain limits (shooting an unarmed man, attacking women and children), and the protocols of confrontation (a fair fight differs from murder). This shared code is what makes the frontier's informal moral community legible, and its violation marks the true villain.
entry_conditions: Multiple characters operating within the frontier world
exit_conditions: The frontier code is invoked, demonstrated, or violated in ways that carry narrative weight; violation of the code is how we identify who deserves to be opposed
typical_variants: Hospitality given to a stranger without question — and the stranger's obligation in return
A man's given word as an unbreakable bond
The distinction between a fair gunfight and a murder
Limits on what you can do even to an enemy — shooting a man in the back marks you as outside the code
failure_modes: No shared code is perceptible — all characters operate according to pure self-interest and this is treated as normal
Code is explicitly stated in dialogue rather than demonstrated through behavior and consequence
Violation of the code has no social consequence — others do not respond to its breach
signals_in_text: Characters make costly gestures of hospitality or fair dealing without explanation — the code is just understood
Violation of the code is recognized and named by other characters
The antagonist's villainy is established partly through code violation, not just raw violence

nodes.label: Showdown / Confrontation Scene
nodes.definition: The Western's defining obligatory scene: a direct, personal confrontation between the protagonist and the primary antagonist in which the conflict is resolved through individual agency — most typically a gunfight, but also a confrontation of will, word, or moral position. The showdown cannot be an accident or a technicality; it must be chosen and faced. It is the moment the story has been building toward, and it must carry the full weight of everything at stake — survival, code, identity, justice.
entry_conditions: All prior constraints and escalation have built toward this point
Protagonist and antagonist have been established in direct opposition
The conflict's stakes are at maximum
exit_conditions: The central conflict is resolved through a direct confrontation that required the protagonist to face it rather than avoid it; the resolution carries moral and physical weight
typical_variants: Classic street duel — two men, one draw
Siege confrontation — outnumbered protagonist against many, forcing a final stand
Moral showdown — protagonist refuses to shoot a disarmed or surrendering enemy, and must hold that line
The confrontation that reveals the villain's true nature under pressure
failure_modes: Confrontation is resolved by accident, by a third party, or by the antagonist's incompetence rather than the protagonist's competence and will
Showdown occurs but neither party is in real danger — the outcome was never in doubt
Protagonist avoids the direct confrontation and the conflict is resolved by proxy
signals_in_text: Scene is built up through anticipation — both parties know it is coming
Physical space and terms of the confrontation are established before it begins
Protagonist's choice to face rather than flee is made explicit or strongly implied

nodes.label: Arrival / Departure Scene
nodes.definition: The Western's structural rhythm is shaped by arrivals and departures — the stranger who rides in, the community that watches them go, the outlaw who disappears into the landscape, the family that finally crosses into a new territory. These scenes are obligatory because they establish and resolve the protagonist's relationship to place, community, and belonging. The arrival scene establishes what the protagonist brings into this world; the departure scene measures what the world cost them or what they leave behind.
entry_conditions: Protagonist enters or exits a community, territory, or relationship that defines the story's central arena
exit_conditions: The scene registers what the arrival or departure means — what changes when the protagonist enters or leaves; it is not merely a logistical transition
typical_variants: The stranger rides into town — the community reads and reacts to his presence
The gunfighter who cannot stay once the job is done — the departure as the price of what he is
The homesteader arriving at the land they intend to claim — the land looks back
The outlaw who must leave the territory before the law closes in
failure_modes: Arrival and departure are logistical transitions only — no meaning is attached, no response is registered
The community does not react to the protagonist's presence — no social reading of who has arrived
Departure resolves nothing — the protagonist simply leaves without consequence or meaning
signals_in_text: Other characters observe and respond to the arrival — something shifts in the community
The landscape is present in the arrival or departure — the journey is not elided
Departure scene acknowledges what is being left — not necessarily in dialogue, but in what the protagonist takes a last look at

nodes.label: Landscape-as-Stakes Scene
nodes.definition: At least one scene must render the landscape as the explicit stakes of the conflict — not just the setting in which conflict happens, but what is fought over, survived through, or lost. This scene makes the frontier's claim on the story legible: it could be a scene in which characters must cross a killing desert to survive, a view of the land that explains why it is worth dying for, a moment of landscape beauty or terror that reframes what the story is about, or the destruction of a landscape that represents a way of life.
entry_conditions: Story's central conflict involves or implicates the land
exit_conditions: At least one scene in which the landscape is foregrounded as more than setting — it becomes the site of meaning, the object of desire or fear, or the measure of what has been lost or gained
typical_variants: The exhausted ride through the desert that becomes a test of will — will or land, one of them breaks
The homesteader's first sight of the land they intend to claim — the land's beauty and hostility registered simultaneously
The destruction of a landscape that has been home — the burning of a ranch, the fencing of open range
The long view from a high place that makes the vastness of the frontier real
failure_modes: Landscape scenes are purely descriptive — beautiful but narratively inert
The land is never the object of anyone's desire, fear, or grief
Landscape scenes could be cut without any effect on the story's meaning
signals_in_text: Characters speak or act toward the landscape as if it matters — as an adversary, a home, a prize
The scene's purpose is to make the land felt, not just seen
What happens in the landscape scene changes what the characters want or what they understand about their situation

nodes.label: Stoic, Spare, Elemental Tone
nodes.definition: The Western demands a tone of stoicism and economy: characters say less than they feel, the prose or narration does not over-explain moral situations, and the elemental realities of survival — heat, distance, hunger, violence, death — are presented with a plainness that respects rather than sentimentalizes them. This tone is not emotional absence; it is emotional compression. The Western's affects are real but underplayed, and their understatement gives them weight. When the stoic Western protagonist finally speaks or acts with feeling, it means something precisely because they so rarely do.
exit_conditions: Prose, dialogue, and narration sustain a register of economy and directness; emotional moments land through restraint rather than elaboration
typical_variants: Laconic protagonist who communicates through action and silence rather than speech
Spare landscape description that gives the environment weight without ornamenting it
Underplayed grief — characters absorb loss without dramatic display, and the audience feels it more for the restraint
Violence described with plainness — no aestheticization, no inflation
failure_modes: Characters explain their moral positions and emotional states at length
Landscape description becomes lush and ornamental — the frontier is romanticized rather than rendered
Grief and loss are handled with melodramatic display — the stoic register collapses into sentiment
signals_in_text: Dialogue is direct and minimal — characters do not over-explain
Significant moments are handled with restraint; the camera or narrative does not linger to tell us how to feel
Physical action carries emotional meaning that is not subsequently explicated in dialogue or narration

nodes.label: Romanticized Violence Without Consequence
nodes.definition: The Western's central failure mode: violence is aestheticized, choreographed, and presented as inherently heroic or glamorous, without registering the physical and moral cost that gives violence its weight in the genre. When the gunfight is beautiful rather than terrible, when the body count is exciting rather than sobering, when the protagonist kills without hesitation or aftermath, the Western has betrayed its own premise — that the frontier's violence is the thing to be reckoned with, not celebrated. This anti-pattern produces a Western that feels like a fantasy rather than a moral argument.
entry_conditions: Violence occurs in a Western context
exit_conditions: Violence carries moral and physical weight — injury matters, killing has aftermath, the protagonist is changed by what they do and witness
typical_variants: Gunfight choreographed as a dance — exciting and bloodless, with no cost
Protagonist kills many enemies without psychological effect across the entire story
Violence treated as the solution to moral problems rather than the price of them
Deaths of named characters passed over without grief or consequence
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence marks a genre violation
signals_in_text: No enemy deaths register as having any human weight
Protagonist draws and fires without hesitation, doubt, or aftermath across the whole narrative
Body count is high but the audience is never asked to feel it
Violence is staged for excitement rather than meaning — tempo is fast, consequences are absent

edges.label: Promise requires frontier setting as an active shaping force
edges.effects_on_stakes: The frontier's hostility and indifference set the baseline level of danger — survival is never guaranteed by the setting itself
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined partly by how they respond to the frontier; competence and resilience are measured against the land's demands
common_alternatives: Contemporary Neo-Western transposes the frontier condition to modern settings while preserving the structural constraint
anti_patterns: Frontier as decorative backdrop — characters never shaped or constrained by it

edges.label: Promise requires personal moral code as the story's operative law
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every moral question in the story is resolved — or failed — by individual conscience rather than institutional authority
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's integrity is the story's primary moral instrument; what they choose defines who they are
common_alternatives: Revisionist Western may complicate or interrogate the code rather than affirm it
anti_patterns: Protagonist has no code — acts on pure opportunism without moral structure

edges.label: Promise requires physical competence to matter and to be tested
edges.effects_on_stakes: Survival is partly a function of skill; the protagonist's capability is a real factor in outcomes
edges.effects_on_character: What the protagonist can do is inseparable from who they are — competence is characterization
common_alternatives: Settler Western may foreground endurance and practical skill over combat skill specifically
anti_patterns: Physical competence is irrelevant — the story's outcomes depend on luck or information, not capability

edges.label: Promise requires that law be absent, weak, or corrupt
edges.effects_on_stakes: The absence of reliable law means that every act of justice or violence is a personal decision with no institutional cover
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot outsource moral decisions to authority — they are their own final court
common_alternatives: The lawman protagonist may represent institutional law, but the law he embodies is his personal code wearing a badge, not a functioning system
anti_patterns: Functioning legal institutions resolve the central conflict — the Western's moral architecture is bypassed

edges.label: Promise requires stoic, spare, elemental tone throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: Spare tone ensures the frontier's weight is felt — the land and its demands are rendered plainly, not romanticized
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' emotional lives are compressed rather than displayed — feeling is present but underplayed
common_alternatives: Revisionist Western may use the stoic register to expose what it costs rather than to glorify it
anti_patterns: Lush, ornamental, or melodramatic tone that sentimentalizes the frontier or inflates its violence

edges.label: Absent law branches into Classic Western where individual virtue fills the vacuum
preconditions: Narrative affirms that individual virtue and competence can restore order
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist becomes the only available instrument of justice; what they do and how they do it is the story's moral center
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's code is essentially adequate to the situation — the Classic Western trusts its hero
common_alternatives: Revisionist Western
Settler Western
Neo-Western
anti_patterns: Classic form without genuine moral test — the hero is never in doubt, never costs

edges.label: Personal code under interrogation branches into Revisionist Western
preconditions: The protagonist's code or the genre's moral clarity is explicitly interrogated or shown to be insufficient
edges.effects_on_stakes: The protagonist's code may be part of the problem — following it may perpetuate injustice rather than remedy it
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's integrity does not protect them from complicity; the Western hero's self-image is shown to have a shadow
common_alternatives: Classic Western
Settler Western
Neo-Western
anti_patterns: Revisionist tone without structural interrogation — just a darker Classic Western

edges.label: Frontier setting as home-to-be-built branches into Settler Western
preconditions: Protagonist is engaged in settlement rather than conflict resolution as itinerant
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are generational — what is built or lost will outlast the protagonist
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's relationship to the land is not transient; they have chosen to belong to this place
common_alternatives: Classic Western
Revisionist Western
Neo-Western
anti_patterns: Settler setting but no genuine stake in the land — protagonist can leave without loss

edges.label: Frontier conditions transposed to contemporary setting branches into Neo-Western
preconditions: Core constraints apply in a contemporary or non-historical setting
Modern world is shown to contain zones where frontier logic operates
edges.effects_on_stakes: Contemporary frontier zones — criminal economies, rural margins, institutional voids — produce the same stakes as the historical frontier
edges.effects_on_character: Modern protagonist navigates the same moral demands as the Western hero, without the historical costume
common_alternatives: Classic Western
Revisionist Western
Settler Western
anti_patterns: Western aesthetic in a contemporary setting but frontier constraints are absent — law functions, institutions help, competence is irrelevant

edges.label: Classic Western requires landscape to function as character
edges.effects_on_stakes: The landscape sets the physical terms of the conflict — distances, terrain, weather are not neutral
edges.effects_on_character: The Classic hero's competence includes mastery of the landscape — reading terrain, managing distance, surviving exposure
common_alternatives: Desert Southwest
Mountain terrain
Open plains as killing ground
anti_patterns: Classic Western in which landscape is decorative — the hero moves through it without being shaped by it

edges.label: Classic Western requires isolation to make individual action consequential
edges.effects_on_stakes: Help is not coming — the protagonist must resolve the conflict with available resources
edges.effects_on_character: Self-reliance is not a preference but a necessity; the lone hero is alone because there is no one else
common_alternatives: Small community that is collectively isolated
Protagonist cut off from allies mid-conflict
anti_patterns: Reinforcements arrive to solve the conflict — the protagonist's isolation was illusory

edges.label: Settler Western requires limited resources and isolation as the conditions of the settlement project
edges.effects_on_stakes: What the settlers have is what they brought — the land does not supplement their needs, and help is a season's ride away
edges.effects_on_character: Settlers must plan for scarcity, endure isolation, and solve problems with what they have rather than what they can summon
common_alternatives: Winter as the great isolator — the homestead is cut off for months
Drought that depletes resources without possibility of outside replenishment
anti_patterns: Settlement story where supply lines are reliable and help arrives whenever needed

edges.label: Revisionist Western requires frontier code as the target of interrogation
edges.effects_on_stakes: The code that the Classic Western treats as the solution is shown to be part of the problem — it enforces an order that serves some and destroys others
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist may discover that their adherence to the code has made them complicit in injustice
common_alternatives: Code is revealed as the ideology of the conqueror
Code is shown to protect only those already powerful
anti_patterns: Revisionist Western that deconstructs everything except the code — the protagonist's personal ethics are exempted from interrogation

edges.label: Neo-Western requires contemporary landscape to function with frontier force
edges.effects_on_stakes: The modern landscape — desert highway, borderland, rural poverty — must impose real constraints, not just look like the West
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist navigates a contemporary environment that has the same indifference and hostility as the historical frontier
common_alternatives: Industrial wasteland as frontier
Drug corridor as lawless territory
Rural hinterland as the landscape beyond institutional reach
anti_patterns: Neo-Western set in environments that are easily traversed and resourced — the frontier condition is absent

edges.label: Landscape determines the physical terms and location of the showdown
edges.effects_on_stakes: The showdown's terrain is not arbitrary — it is determined by the landscape logic of the story and shapes who has advantage
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's mastery of the landscape is part of what brings them to the showdown on adequate terms
common_alternatives: Showdown in a canyon the protagonist knows better than the antagonist
Showdown in an open street where neither has cover
Showdown at the antagonist's stronghold — terrain favors the villain
anti_patterns: Showdown location is arbitrary — the landscape has no bearing on the confrontation

edges.label: Isolation ensures the showdown cannot be avoided — no help is coming
preconditions: Isolation and limited resources established as setting conditions
edges.effects_on_stakes: The showdown must be faced because there is no alternative — no retreat to safety, no outside rescue, no appeal to authority
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist chooses to face the confrontation knowing there is no fallback — the isolation makes the choice definitive
common_alternatives: Protagonist could escape but chooses to stay and face the conflict
anti_patterns: Showdown is avoided because help arrives at the last moment — the isolation was false

edges.label: Personal code requires payoff in the showdown
preconditions: Protagonist's code has been established and tested throughout the story
edges.effects_on_stakes: The showdown is the moment the code is fully enacted — the protagonist demonstrates through action what they stand for
edges.effects_on_character: How the protagonist conducts the showdown — what they will and will not do — is the code's ultimate expression
common_alternatives: Code requires refusing a killing shot on a disarmed enemy
Code requires facing the enemy openly rather than ambushing them
Code requires completing the confrontation even when retreat is available
anti_patterns: Showdown is won through a code violation — protagonist shoots first without warning, shoots in the back, uses deception that contravenes their established principles

edges.label: Frontier code governs how arrivals and departures are conducted and read
preconditions: Frontier code established as operating social reality
edges.effects_on_stakes: How a stranger arrives — armed or sheathed, alone or with others, willing to identify themselves — signals intent and code; the community reads it
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's arrival enacts their code — how they present themselves, what they ask for, how they respond to hospitality or suspicion
common_alternatives: The arrival that violates the code signals danger — community is right to be wary
The departure that honors the code — protagonist repays hospitality before leaving
anti_patterns: Arrival and departure are logistical with no social reading — the code is not present in these transitions

edges.label: Landscape-as-character requires at least one scene where land is the explicit stakes
preconditions: Landscape has been established as a shaping force throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: The land must be made the object of desire, grief, or survival — not just the space in which these things occur
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's relationship to the land is clarified — what they are willing to endure or sacrifice for it
common_alternatives: The first sight of a homestead claim
The crossing of a killing desert as proof of belonging
The burning of the ranch as the destruction of everything built
anti_patterns: Landscape is never the object of feeling — it is only ever the background of action

edges.label: Physical competence is tested and proven in the showdown
preconditions: Protagonist's physical competence has been established and demonstrated throughout
edges.effects_on_stakes: The showdown's outcome is determined by skill, not by luck or by the antagonist's incompetence — the protagonist's competence is why they survive
edges.effects_on_character: What the protagonist has been shown to be capable of throughout the story is the thing that brings them through the climactic confrontation
common_alternatives: Tactical intelligence rather than speed of draw
Knowledge of the terrain that enables the protagonist to choose the confrontation's terms
Skill under pressure versus skill in calm conditions — the showdown tests the harder version
anti_patterns: Showdown won by luck, by the antagonist's mistake, or by a newly introduced resource — protagonist's established competence is irrelevant to the resolution

edges.label: Absence of genuine moral code produces romanticized violence without consequence
preconditions: Violence occurs but the protagonist has no code that gives it moral weight
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without a code, violence has no stakes beyond spectacle — winning or losing are equally meaningless
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is a killing machine rather than a moral agent — their violence is exciting rather than meaningful
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes the genre's central failure mode
